coredata.dc:title: Quantum dots and carbon dots based fluorescent sensors for TB biomarkers detection
coredata.dc:creator: Bhattacharyya, Dhiman
Sarswat, Prashant K.
Free, Michael L.
coredata.dc:description: The clinical use of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) detection for improved and rapid diagnoses of various diseases possesses a significant potential for the correct and timely screening of such diseases. Tuberculosis (TB) is a major pulmonary disease of concern, and can potentially be diagnosed using breath analysis techniques. Various sensing methods have been demonstrated and utilized to detect TB-VOCs. Some of the methods are based on electrical signal or change in resistance of device due to VOCs while some methods utilize mass spectroscopy. A low cost and quick method is very essential at this stage. Hence, in this paper, we report the synthesis of stable colloidal suspensions of CdSe QDs as well as carbon dots as a viable photoluminescent platform for detection of TB biomarkers. Such novel CdSe/carbon dots based sensing solution with tunable excitation and emission properties acts as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of TB biomarkers. The development of a smart, tunable fluorescent sensor system can provide an economically feasible solution and pave the way for rapid disease diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Biomarkers
Sensors
Tuberculosis
Colorimetry

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on European Materials Research Society Fall Meeting 2016 (EMRS_Fall2016_D); Edited by Yogendra Mishra, Arul Natarajan, Jost Adam and Jani Kotakoski

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General methods for VOB detection and colorimetry approach
Measurement of optical response of TB VOBs treated QDs/CDs solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A diagram (schematic) showing proposed method of TB biomarker detection using quantum dots. The methodology involves the collection of breath into a Tedlar bag and subsequent mixing into a QDs (or C-dot) solution. The spectral analysis will be performed for estimation of VOB concentration and prediction of patient health.
Emission spectra of quantum dots of different size recorded at different wavelength excitation. S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 are different set of quantum dots with increasing size.

Emission behavior of different sets of QDs in response to different wavelength excitation. It can be seen that for one particular size the shift in emission peak is negligible when excitation wavelength increases.
Overall emission peak position-excitation peak relationship for different sized quantum dots. It can be seen that emission behavior is almost independent of excitation wavelength.

(a) Emission, (b) absorbance spectra, and (c) digital photographs during illumination: CdSe quantum dots; QDs with pyridine; and QDs-nicotinate mixture. It can be seen that nicotinate –QDs solution can be easily distinguished from pristine QDs solution.
Estimated energy band diagram showing relative position of conduction and valence band of CdSe QDs as well as Pyridine and Nicotinate.

(a) Absorbance and (b) emission spectra of pristine carbon dots and with different concentrations of VOBs. It can be seen that nicotinate – CDs solution can be easily distinguished from pristine CDs solution using UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: Quantum dots and carbon dots based fluorescent sensors for TB biomarkers detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical Engineering, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, UT 84112, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 135 S, 1460 E, Room 412, Salt Lake city, UT 84112, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The clinical use of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) detection for improved and rapid diagnoses of various diseases possesses a significant potential for the correct and timely screening of such diseases. Tuberculosis (TB) is a major pulmonary disease of concern, and can potentially be diagnosed using breath analysis techniques. Various sensing methods have been demonstrated and utilized to detect TB-VOCs. Some of the methods are based on electrical signal or change in resistance of device due to VOCs while some methods utilize mass spectroscopy. A low cost and quick method is very essential at this stage. Hence, in this paper, we report the synthesis of stable colloidal suspensions of CdSe QDs as well as carbon dots as a viable photoluminescent platform for detection of TB biomarkers. Such novel CdSe/carbon dots based sensing solution with tunable excitation and emission properties acts as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of TB biomarkers. The development of a smart, tunable fluorescent sensor system can provide an economically feasible solution and pave the way for rapid disease diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid, point-of-care (POC) disease diagnostics presents several challenges including cost, detection time, equipment portability, and performance. Detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) based disease biomarkers from the breath provides a potential solution to the aforementioned problems [1]. The clinical use of VOCs identification for improved and an accurate diagnoses of pulmonary [2,3] and non-pulmonary [4–7] diseases possesses a significant potential for the rapid screening of such diseases. Tuberculosis (TB) is a major pulmonary disease of concern, which can potentially be diagnosed via breath analysis. In a recent report, world health organization (WHO) states that almost 9.6 million people worldwide are infected with TB each year, while ∼3 million people do not get the basic care they need and ∼1.5 million people die of the disease, causing TB to be the leading cause of death worldwide alongside HIV [8]. A VOCs detection based approach that falls under the “moderate complexity assays” group for TB diagnostics was presented [9]. In addition to the capability of giant African pouch rats (apopo) for sniffing TB, the technologies enlisted for VOC based approach include TB Breathalyzer (Rapid Biosensor Systems), Breathlink (Menssana), Aeonose (The eNose Company), Breath analysis instrument (Metabolomx), and Prototype Breathalyzer (Next Dimensions Tech). The TB Breathalyzer utilizes the evanescent wave technology leading to reduction in fluorescent signal when the TB antigen displaces fluorescently coated analogues and bonds to the antibody in the device [10]. Breathlink identifies the markers of oxidative stress and the disease using proprietary algorithms where alveolar breath VOC samples are separated by gas chromatography and detected with flame ionization (GC-FID) or surface acoustic wave detection (GC-SAW) with high sensitivity [11]. The Aeonose utilizes the basic concept of an electronic nose, or machine olfaction, where a measurement generates complex multi-dimensional data for each measurement combined with a pattern recognition technique that interprets the complex data and relates it to a target value or class [12]. Metabolomx uses a colorimetric sensor array and breath analysis instrument. The colorimetric sensors help to capture the chemical signature pattern of the complex mixture of VOC's present in breath thereby helping in identifying tuberculosis, colon cancer, and lung cancer detection [13]. It is evident that although the aforementioned technologies fall under the VOC based detection category, none of them effectively detects the specific VOCs found in the exhaled breath of TB patients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General methods for VOB detection and colorimetry approach
ce:section.ce:para: The prominent volatile organic biomarkers (VOBs) in the breath of smear positive tuberculosis patients have been detected using solid phase microextraction fibers with GC-MS analysis [14,15]. Recently, the capability of electrochemical detection of these predominate TB VOBs using metal functionalized titania nanotubes array based sensing platform has been demonstrated [1,16]. Although such sensors provide an excellent platform for rapid and non-invasive detection, sensitivity might be an issue in the case of extremely low biomarker concentrations, which is critical for early detection or latent TB subjects. Colorimetric sensors for VOCs and odor detection and quantification have been a topic of great interest, due to their increased sensitivity correlated to change in color or fluorescence even at low concentrations of the VOBs [17–22].

Colorimetric assays [23] warrant inevitable mention of a critical component, quantum dots. Quantum dot nanocomposites have been utilized for the delivery of both small and large molecular weight therapeutics [24], as well as for sensing applications. Fluorescence assisted quantum dot (QD) based protein detection has been utilized for sensing of the multiple cancer biomarkers [25,26]. CdS quantum dots can bind effectively to methyl viologen molecules [27]. In addition, EDA/EDTA modified graphene quantum dots based fluorescent sensor has demonstrated promising capabilities for the detection of dipicolinic acid, an important anthrax biomarker [28]. In other research, Cu-Zn-Sn based chalcogenide-quantum dots tethered to a rhodamine-fluorene derivative have shown immense potential for selective detection of VOBs specific to lung cancer [29]. Amine terminated gold nanodendrites sensitized quantum dots (AuND-QDs) assembly has been successfully employed for sensing of highly explosive and environmentally detrimental trinitrotoluene (TNT) [30]. Therefore, it is evident that the development of a quantum dot based fluorescence platform is particularly attractive for application in biological labeling, disease detection, drug delivery, and explosive compound monitoring. Also, it is important to note that for most sensing applications, the quantum dots are coupled with a ligand which can selectively bind with the biomarker of interest and render fluorescence at the same time. The predominate TB biomarkers can be classified as methyl esters. Zhang et al had demonstrated that modified metalloporphyrins can catalytically oxidize fatty acid methyl esters [31]. Also, protoporphyrin IX dimethyl ester has been demonstrated to be a potent photosensitizer of human nasopharyngeal carcinoma. In addition, metalloporphyrins have been declared as nearly ideal for detection of metal ligating vapors facilitated by their open co-ordination sites for axial ligation [19]. Further, large spectral shifts upon ligand binding, intense coloration, together with their ability to provide differentiation based on metal selective coordination, renders metalloporphyrins indispensable for VOCs detection [19]. Although the QD - metalloporphyrin nanohybrid photo-label seems viable, its specificity towards the methyl ester based TB biomarkers might be low. Therefore, we suggest an alternative QD based photo-label with higher selectivity and specificity towards the biomarkers.

For biological applications, QDs typically utilize ZnS as the outermost shell because of its role as a physical barrier to prevent potentially toxic core materials such as CdSe from leaching out to the surrounding environments [32]. In addition, the higher band gap of ZnS (∼3.7 eV) confines the excitons to the cores and prevents them from interacting with the surrounding environment, thereby maintaining high fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) [32]. These QDs exhibit a tendency to aggregate in the absence of any ligands. The ligands essentially provide enhanced functional stability in a cooperative binded ensemble. In this regard, polymeric ligands appended with functional pendant groups render significantly enriched functionality to the QDs. Among the more common chemical groups able to coordinate with Zn at the QD surface, thiols form reasonably stable bonds, leading to their ubiquitous use as the surface anchoring group on many discrete QD ligands. Polymeric ligands appended with multiple thiols as pendant groups yield robust hydrophilic QDs [32]. Thiolated ligands, especially the monothiolated variety, are prone to oxidation in air and under light with the presence of QDs potentially contributing to this as a photocatalyst. On the other hand multidentate thiolated ligands are extremely susceptible to disulfide formation and cross-linking, thereby reducing their functionality. Researchers have shown that pyridines demonstrate excellent surface binding capabilities and can be utilized as ligand anchors with QDs in place of thiols. Pyridine is well-known for use in replacing the original hydrophobic ligands on the QD surfaces and has been extensively utilized to fabricate QD-based thin film devices such as solar cells [33]. Also, Pyridine is a good electron donor due to the presence of a pair of isolated nitrogen electrons that are delocalized over its entire aromatic ring [34]. CdSe quantum dots show a good electron affinity, meaning that the nitrogen readily coordinates with them to form a stable complex. In addition Guo et al. [35] showed pyridine coatings of the QDs was essential for obtaining densely packed monolayers of QDs with minimum clumps over large areas of graphene.

Here, we report the synthesis of stable colloidal suspensions of CdSe QDs as well as carbon dots as a viable photoluminescent platform for detection of TB biomarkers. Such novel CdSe/Carbon quantum dots based sensing solution with tunable excitation and emission properties acts as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of TB biomarkers. The development of a smart, tunable fluorescent sensor system can provide an economically feasible solution and pave the way for rapid disease diagnostics. We have presented preliminary results including emission, absorbance, and binding behavior of selected TB VOBs with QDs and CDs.

For synthesis of CdSe quantum dots, 15 mg of cadmium oxide was first dissolved in 0.5 ml of oleic acid and 10–12 ml of 1-octadecene. This mixture was gently heated in order to completely dissolve the cadmium oxide and to form cadmium oleate. Stirring was continued until reaching a temperature of ∼225 °C. Prior to preparation of cadmium based precursor, synthesis of selenium precursor is essential. For such synthesis 30 mg of selenium powder was mixed with a solution containing 5 ml of 1-octadecene and 0.5 ml of trioctylphosphine. This solution was heated and stirred for dissolution of selenium. The full dissolution of selenium resulted in a transparent solution. This precursor was stored for CdSe dots preparation. The selenium precursor (∼1 ml) was injected on a bath containing cadmium oleate precursor that was kept at ∼ 225 °C. Note that growth of CdSe QDs started soon after mixing. A small amount of QDs mixture was quickly withdrawn and the process was repeated at predermined intervals. In this way, QDs of different size can be prepared and named as S1, S2, S3, S4, and S5. The size of QDs was as follows: S1 < S2 < S3 < S4 < S5.

Carbon dots were synthesized using a carbonaceous source. For CDs synthesis, 15 ml of a drink containing sugar, was mixed with an appropriate amount of ethanol and oleic acid. Such mixture was gently heated and monitored for ∼1.5 h at a temperature of ∼225° C. The nucleation and growth was controlled by reducing the temperature. It was observed that, the reaction mixture was clear in the beginning of reaction which finally converted into a light yellow transparent semisolid. At this stage irradiation of UV light in the reaction flask showed yellow-green emission. At this stage the reaction was terminated and sample was diluted with absolute ethanol. Other details regarding CDs synthesis was discussed in our earlier report [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of optical response of TB VOBs treated QDs/CDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: Optical characterizations were conducted using an Ocean Optics spectrophotometer and an Avantes spectrophotometer in the range 300–800 nm using quartz cuvettes. For emission spectra, a range of excitation wavelength (360–650 nm) was utilized. A schematic diagram for proposed system for VOBs detection is represented in Fig. 1 . In order to evaluate VOBs concentration, first breath sample was collected into a tedlar bag. The breath on this gas sampling bag will be liberated into the QDs based sensing solution. An automated system based on change in spectral response will monitor the presence of VOBs in breath and health status. In this research the authors used 10 mM solution of methyl nicotinate (in ethyl alcohol) and was mixed in equal amount of QDs (or CDs). In order to mimic breath VOBs, nitrogen gas can be bubbled to methyl nicotinate solution and resulting vapor can be transformed to QDs/CDs solution. The authors has estimated that in case of nitrogen flow rate of ∼200 sccm, the resulting VOBs concentration in sensing chamber was ∼ 2 mM [1]. Hence, authors performed few experiments at relative lower concentration.

Fig. 2 (a–e) shows an excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of quantum dots of different sizes. We have shown five different graphs (each for specific excitation) and associated emission behavior. It can be seen that emission peaks corresponding to big size QDs exhibit a redshift with respect to small size QDs. The normalized spectra for excitation source can be seen in our earlier report [36]. Fig. 3 shows emission behavior of different sets of QDs in response to different wavelength excitation. From Fig. 3, we have emission peak position using Gaussian fit for each set of excitation. Fig. 4 shows an excitation dependent emission peak position relationship for different QDs. It can be seen that emission peak position for each set of QDs does not depend much on excitation wavelength. Most of the curves are almost flat. However, it can be seen that with increase in size the emission peak shifts towards higher wavelength. A difference between emission behaviors of other nanocrystals such as C-dots and QDs is evident in these experiments. In the case of C-dots, emission maxima greatly rely upon the excitation wavelength and such a dependency was more effective in the case of small size QDs [36]. In order to examine the QDs-nicotinate interactions, the emission and absorbance spectra of QDs-nicotinate mixture was examined (see Fig. 5 a–b). A significant change in emission behavior was observed when nicotinate solution was mixed with QDs. One set of QDs (size_4) the emission peak exhibited a blue shift ∼ 60 nm after nicotinate solution mixing. It can be seen that integrated absorbance of mixture is significantly less than pristine QDs. The absorbance of only nicotinate solution mainly lies in the deep UV region. The band edge of pristine QDs was around ∼420 nm, however, a 100 nm decrease in wavelength was observed for the QDs-nicotinate mixture. We have also presented a digital photograph of QDs with and without VOBs (see Fig. 5c). It can be seen that quantum dots with methyl nicotinate solution appears dark brown and very distinct from QDs solution. During illumination QDs-methyl nicotinate solution exhibits completely different emission compare to pristine QDs.

Although, in our analysis it was demonstrated that the sensing of the TB biomarkers follows an excitation-independent emission process, it will further be pertinent in the context of this paper to explain the mechanism of photoluminescence. Developing a good understanding of the photoluminescence origins will aid in the development of carbon/quantum dot materials with tunable excitation-emission characteristics for advanced sensing applications and beyond.

Proposed theories predict the differences in the particle size, surface traps, and the intramolecular hydrogen bonds, among others to name a few, as the possible mechanisms contributing to possible quantum effects giving rise to varied excitation-emission characteristics. But one of the factors, which at times get overlooked, is the presence or the formation of the intermediate aromatic compounds during the reaction of a target with the quantum dots, which can lead to a change in electron recombination behavior as well as the formation of sub-surface traps that can greatly affect the photoluminescence characteristics.

Fig. 4 re-affirms the particle size based concept as the emission wavelength increases with particle size for the same excitation wavelength. Such a behavior is different for carbon dots where the presence of the surface trap states leads to an excitation-dependent emission [37] characteristics [36]. However, irrespective of nature of the dots, to achieve an enhanced sensitivity in the detection of such disease biomarkers through photoluminescence, the quantum/carbon dot solution should be homogenous in nature, consisting of same-sized particles. For such uniformly sized quantum dots, an excitation-independent emission will be the predominant photoluminescence mode and any change in intensity can be attributed to the difference in the concentration of the biomarkers. Methyl nicotinate is the most prominent TB biomarker and is known to be present in the breath sample of affected patients in almost 100% of the cases. Methyl nicotinate is a nicotinic acid methyl ester which has a nitrogen atom embedded in the aromatic ring. It was illustrated that the nitrogen containing organic compounds, especially with the short chains, act as effective passivating agents, which indirectly determines the photoluminescence character [38]. At extremely high temperatures, such passivating agents are believed to be rendered unstable leading to the greater number of trap states, and thus excitation-dependent luminescence behavior. In our case, both quantum and carbon dots were synthesized around 220°C, which is considered to be moderately high temperature. Synthesis of the quantum dots at moderately high temperatures may have rendered the nitrogen-bearing passivating agents intact on the surface of the quantum/carbon dots, leading to the emissions which were independent of the excitation wavelength, as observed in Fig. 4. Further, a solution containing homogenously sized quantum dots, will have less or negligible contribution from the differences in surface trap states. For a particular size of the homogenous QD solution, the excitation energy is higher than the differences in the energy gap, and thus the emission is excitation-independent. The predominant energy gap transitions plays a major role as the QD size changes.

But in most of the sensing methodology, selectivity is as important as sensitivity. This is essential in ruling out false positives during the detection procedure. To establish the characteristic emission peak of the QD-VOB interaction and to rule out the detection of other nitrogen bearing aromatic compounds using our quantum dots, experiments were performed with pyridine as well. Pyridine is a heterocyclic organic compound where a methine group is substituted with a nitrogen atom in the aromatic lattice, and thus possess a structure similar to that of methyl nicotinate. But the subtle differences in chemical structure introduces surface state and transition mode based emission levels between the conduction (HOMO) band and valence (LUMO) band of nicotinate and pyridine. It is when these specific transition levels are excited corresponding to their excitation wavelengths, excitation-dependent emission takes place. Fig. 5a illustrates that characteristic emission peak for CdSe – Pyridine mixture (excitation ∼525 nm) is slightly red-shifted in comparison to the CdSe-Nicotinate mixture (excitation ∼505 nm) peak. This is consistent with the subtle differences in energy band gap of pyridine and methyl nicotinate. As the excitation energy was lower than the difference in the energy band gap transitions, the energy gap transitions coupled with surface trap states transitions contributed to the excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior.

One of the differences between QDs-VOB and CDs-VOB interactions is the formation of the coordination bond between pyridine moiety and metal ion along the plane of aromatic ring [35]. In this case other organic ligands try to stay away from the inorganic nanocrystals surface. A clear broadening of spectral feature is evident (see Fig. 5a), and can be correlated to electronic coupling between individual QDs. We have assumed the optical band gap of CdSe ∼2.0 eV and based on this information, an energy band diagram is plotted (see Fig. 6 ). Pyridine acts as a hole scavenger for CdSe type QDs and it is validated that QDs-pyridine system exhibits a charge transfer excited state where holes lie on the pyridine ligands. The transient absorbance kinetics (TAK) for such system can be linked to the radical-cation transient of the ligand [35]. For methyl nicotinate, such kinetics is expected to be relatively different than pyridine and can be seen in our preliminary results. The relative band position and band offset was different for these compounds and hence, photo-induced charge transfer and bleach recovery was different as evident in spectroscopic analysis. It has been reported that TOPO and surface Cd atoms bond together involving the lone electron pair lying on the oxygen atom [39]. Here formal charges on the P and O atoms are zero. Such ligands have a tendency to confine the charge carriers within the core of the nanocrystals. Pyridine and Cd atoms bond together involving the lone pair of nitrogen. Pyridine (or its derivative) resonance structures due to their π ring contribute to trapping holes. It is helpful for photoexcited holes transport from the semiconductor dot-core. Hence, it is anticipated that pyridine derivative also exhibits different behavior from the pristine QDs.

The effect of interactions of VOB with C-dots was also examined. We have chosen methyl nicotinate and methyl –p-anisate solution (10 mM) and change in emission and absorbance properties was examined. It can be seen that emission intensity reduces significantly after addition of VOBs (See Fig. 7 ). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of pristine QDs and with methyl nicotinate biomarkers in Fig. 7a demonstrate a typical absorption band located at ∼375 nm which can be attributed to the contribution of surface groups and the π-π* transitions. The strong and symmetric absorption band at ∼375 nm is prominent due to the presence of nitrogen containing nicotinate groups, which are efficient electron donors that enhance the passivation of the carbon dots. Also, by varying the concentration of the biomarkers, it was observed (see Fig. 7b) that the emission intensity decreases as the concentration increases. It can be possibly due to the non-radiative electron transfer caused by the unusual coordination interaction between the nitrogen atoms and the carbon dots leading to fluorescence quenching.

Note that CDs have advantages including high chemical inertness, water compatibility, and biocompatibility. A quenching effect can be seen when methyl nicotinate or methyl anisate is added to the C-dots solution in ethyl alcohol. The exact mechanism of such quenching is not known, however, it is believed that functional units on the surface of CDs act as an electron acceptors and induce such quenching [40]. We have also observed that fluorescence intensity is time dependent and reduces after some time. Most of these experiments have been performed at neutral pH, however pH can alter the fluorescence efficiency and quenching capabilities. Earlier, a fluorescent chemosensor for pyridine detection was studied [41]. It was observed that N-doped CDs exhibit enhancement of emission peak intensity during addition of pyridine. At a certain range of pH N-doped CDs interacts with pyridine and such interaction was specific for carboxylate group on the surface of CDs and the N+-H group in pyridine [41]. Hence, a complex formation based on electrostatic interaction is considered as a factor responsible for energy transfer and higher fluorescence. In contrast, the presence of functional groups in methyl nicotinate alters its optical activity. The presence of two similar group in CDs and methyl nicotinate, respectively, is believed to be a key reason for reduced electrostatic interactions. Hence in this case, the reduction in emission peak intensity during increasing VOBs concentration can be easily linked.

In summary, we have presented preliminary results for detection of tuberculosis volatile organic biomarkers (VOBs) using quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs). The QDs and CDs have been synthesized using standard methods and were used without any functionalization or capping. Both emission and absorbance properties of CDs and QDs were found to be significantly different than VOB modified CDs/QDs. The visual inspection suggested a completely different color when VOBs were mixed with these fluorescent nanocrystals. The QDs exhibited a shift in emission peak whereas CDs showed a reduction in peak intensity when VOBs were mixed within it. The transient absorbance kinetics for these systems are different due to the presence of metal-organic bonding in QDs. Overall, an algorithm can be created for correlating VOBs concentration and measureable properties of spectral features.
